# Code Insights — Auto Memory

## Agent Spawning Constraint (CRITICAL)
- Sub-agents (PM, TA, Dev) CANNOT spawn other agents via the Task tool
- Only the orchestrator (main Claude session) can spawn teammates
- PM must send SPAWN_REQUEST messages to orchestrator when it needs agents
- Fixed in `.claude/commands/start-feature.md` on 2026-02-27

## Product Vision
- Free, open-source, local-first tool helping developers analyze AI coding sessions
- Personal learning tool. No team/org features. No cloud sync. No monetization.
- Multi-source: Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, VS Code Copilot Chat (5 providers)
- Architecture: Single-repo pnpm monorepo (CLI + Dashboard SPA + Hono server)
- Database: SQLite at `~/.code-insights/data.db`, Schema V7 (V8 planned for native analysis)

## Native Analysis Feature (Phase 12, v4.8.0) — IN PROGRESS
- [project_native_analysis_feature.md](project_native_analysis_feature.md) — Zero-config insights via Claude Code hooks
- Plan: `docs/plans/2026-03-28-native-analysis-hooks.md`

## User Preferences (CRITICAL)
- **Always use Subagent-Driven execution** — never ask "which approach?" when executing plans. Default to subagent-driven (this session) over parallel sessions unless the user explicitly requests otherwise.
- **Always use `/start-feature` for big changes** — multi-file features (3+ files, cross-package changes) MUST go through `/start-feature` with full ceremony.
- **NO MVPs, NO PROTOTYPES** — product is LIVE. Always design and implement the FULL solution.
- **Quality bar is HIGH** — no mediocre results. Simple, clean, effective. Don't over-engineer.
- **Ask, don't assume** — when in doubt about an approach or decision, ask the user.
- **Code reviews include LLM expert** — conditional 4th reviewer when PR touches `server/src/llm/`, LLM API calls, structured output schemas, or token budgets.

## Project Conventions
- Dashboard types use snake_case matching SQLite/API responses
- FOUC prevention script in index.html must stay in sync with ThemeProvider
- Radix UI Sheet requires SheetDescription for ARIA compliance

## SQLite Migration Pattern
- `applyV1` = base schema via SCHEMA_SQL, `applyV2+` = incremental changes
- ALWAYS use `INSERT OR IGNORE INTO schema_version` (not bare INSERT) — UNIQUE constraint on version column
- New indexes/schema elements after V1 should live ONLY in their applyVN function (not in SCHEMA_SQL)
- Migration functions must be in chronological order in migrate.ts (applyV2, applyV3, applyV4, applyV5)
- Current version: **V7** (V6: compact_count/auto_compact_count/slash_commands, V7: analysis_usage table)

## Related Repos (Cross-Repo References)
- **code-insights-web** (marketing, docs, blog): `../code-insights-web/`
- **batonship** (separate project): `../../batonship/batonship/`
- All Claude Code sessions for code-insights run from `code-insights/` dir, not web repo.

## Dynamic Review Specialists (PR #227, 2026-03-21)
- [project_dynamic_review_specialists.md](project_dynamic_review_specialists.md) — Static reviewers replaced with dynamic domain specialists + Runtime Verification Rule

## Docs Structure (Updated 2026-03-21)
- CLAUDE.md restructured as quick reference (~250 lines) with links to sub-docs
- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` — repo structure, data flow, providers, SQLite schema, types, API routes, pages
- `docs/AGENTS.md` — agent suite, ceremony, team workflow, code review, delegation
- `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` — branch discipline, hookify, verification, version bump, config, dev notes
- `docs/PRODUCT.md` — product features, source tools, insight categories, export, reflect
- `docs/VISION.md` — philosophy, phase history, non-goals
- `docs/ROADMAP.md` — phase milestones, version table
- `docs/REVIEW-SPECIALISTS.md` — domain specialist registry, selection algorithm, Runtime Verification Rule
- `docs/plans/` — only pending plans (gamification-shareable-badges.md)
- 12 stale plan files deleted (completed PRs #108-#118, launch content)

## Telemetry Model
- PostHog fully integrated in CLI + Dashboard (not "deferred")
- Opt-out model (enabled by default), NOT opt-in
- 14 event types, stable anonymous device ID (SHA-256)
- Respects CODE_INSIGHTS_TELEMETRY_DISABLED and DO_NOT_TRACK env vars

## Hook Enforcement (PR #217)
- [feedback_ci_gate_enforcement.md](feedback_ci_gate_enforcement.md) — PR creation requires build + test pass
- [feedback_hook_patterns.md](feedback_hook_patterns.md) — Hookify rules ARE real hooks, regex anchoring, JSON protocol, zsh compat

## Key Lessons Learned
- Client-side Mermaid in Next.js static sites is unreliable — pre-render diagrams as static images
- Tailwind v4 + shadcn/ui requires: `@custom-variant dark`, `@theme inline`, `@import "tw-animate-css"`
- `@hono/node-server` and `hono` must be in `cli/package.json` deps for npm-installed users
- After merging dashboard/server changes, must run `pnpm build` from repo root

## Reflect Feature — Pattern Normalization (PR #125)
- 8 canonical effective pattern categories: structured-planning, incremental-implementation, verification-workflow, systematic-debugging, self-correction, context-gathering, domain-expertise, effective-tooling
- Pattern normalizer at `server/src/llm/pattern-normalize.ts` (mirrors friction-normalize.ts)
- Normalization at write time in `saveFacetsToDb()` + belt-and-suspenders at read time
- Confidence >= 50 filter for effective patterns at aggregation time in SQL
- Dashboard: category heading + description sub-items (cap 3 + "+N more")
- `category: string` is REQUIRED on EffectivePattern (no backward compat — Reflect not yet released)

## Reflect Feature — Friction Taxonomy Revision (PR #127)
- 9 canonical friction categories (down from 15): wrong-approach, knowledge-gap, stale-assumptions, incomplete-requirements, context-loss, scope-creep, repeated-mistakes, documentation-gap, tooling-limitation
- 11 legacy aliases in `friction-normalize.ts` remap old categories to new taxonomy
- **Attribution field** on friction points: `"user-actionable" | "ai-capability" | "environmental"`
  - Captures WHO contributed to friction (user behavior vs AI capability vs external)
  - Progress feature will filter on `attribution = 'user-actionable'` only
  - Evidence-based decision tree in prompt guidance (not counterfactual)
- Neutral category descriptions (no blame — attribution carries the WHO)
- Contrastive classification guidance with diagnostic questions per category
- Outdated detection: UNION query flags missing `attribution` OR missing `category`
- Dashboard: friction bar chart → category+description list (matches patterns layout)
- Friction-to-Pattern affinity map designed (e.g., stale-assumptions → context-gathering)
- Environmental categories (documentation-gap, tooling-limitation) excluded from Progress tracking

## Reflect Feature — Effective Pattern Taxonomy Revision (PR #129) ✅
- LLM expert audit confirmed all 8 categories, no additions/removals/renames
- **`driver` field**: `'user-driven' | 'ai-driven' | 'collaborative'` (optional during transition)
- Upgraded classification guidance (~800 tokens): contrastive pairs, in-session signals, "NOT this category" exclusions, driver decision tree, collaborative tiebreaker
- Key disambiguation rule: context-gathering vs domain-expertise — if AI investigated first (Read/Grep), it's context-gathering
- `self-correction` = typically ai-driven, `domain-expertise` = typically ai-driven
- Outdated detection: UNION query flags missing `driver` OR missing `category` (combined arm with OR)
- Driver value validation: console.warn for unrecognized values in parseAnalysisResponse
- Dashboard: dominant driver badge (blue=user, violet=AI, green=collaborative)
- Affinity map DEFERRED to Progress PR (dead code with no consumer)

## Reflect Feature — Weekly ISO Week Navigation (PR #132) ✅
- Replaced 7d/30d/90d/all sliding window with ISO week-based navigation (`2026-W10`)
- `parseIsoWeek()` + `formatIsoWeek()` canonical in `server/src/routes/shared-aggregation.ts`
- Dashboard copies (`getCurrentIsoWeek`, `parseIsoWeekBounds`) have "keep in sync" / "adapted from" comments
- New `GET /api/reflect/weeks` endpoint returns last 8 weeks with session counts + snapshot status (2 bulk queries)
- New `WeekSelector` component: arrows, week label, dot indicators, keyboard nav
- CLI: `--period` removed, replaced with `--week <YYYY-WNN>` (default: current ISO week, validated regex + range 1-53 + year 2020-2100)
- `MIN_FACETS_FOR_REFLECT` lowered from 20 → 8 for weekly scope
- Old `7d/30d/90d` snapshot rows orphaned in DB (harmless, no migration needed)
- `reflect_snapshots.period` column is TEXT — accepts any string, no schema change

## Prompt Quality Taxonomy Revision (PR #136) ✅
- 7 deficit categories: `vague-request`, `missing-context`, `late-constraint`, `unclear-correction`, `scope-drift`, `missing-acceptance-criteria`, `assumption-not-surfaced`
- 3 strength categories: `precise-request`, `effective-context`, `productive-correction`
- **Two-layer output**: takeaways (user-facing before/after on Insights page) + findings (categorized for Reflect aggregation)
- **5 dimension scores** (0-100): `context_provision`, `request_specificity`, `scope_management`, `information_timing`, `correction_quality`
- Normalizer at `server/src/llm/prompt-quality-normalize.ts` (mirrors friction/pattern normalizers, 44 aliases)
- Dashboard utils at `dashboard/src/lib/prompt-quality-utils.ts` (labels + strength set, "keep in sync" with server)
- `CategoryBadge` uses finding's `type` prop as truth, falls back to `getPQCategoryType()`
- `bullets` column stores `[]` for new-schema PQ insights — takeaways live in `metadata.takeaways`
- Backward compat: dual-read `efficiency_score ?? efficiencyScore` in 4 dashboard consumers
- Schema detection: `Array.isArray(metadata.findings)` → new schema, else legacy
- `correction_quality` defaults to 75 when no corrections needed (prompt instruction)
- Agentic/delegation session edge case in classification guidance
- `[pq-monitor]` console.warn for missing categories and unexpected type values
- Dormant `antiPatterns` param removed from `generateRulesSkillsPrompt` in reflect-prompts.ts
- `scope-drift` (PQ) ↔ `scope-creep` (friction) cross-taxonomy mapping deferred to Progress PR

## Reflect Feature — Attribution Rewrite (PR #138) ✅
- Actor-neutral friction category definitions (removed "AI..." subject from all 9 categories)
- 3-step CoT attribution decision tree: External? → User-preventable? → AI capability
- "Lean user-actionable when mixed" tiebreaker maintained
- User infrastructure recognition in pattern driver tree (checked FIRST): CLAUDE.md → user request → AI autonomous → collaborative
- Baseline exclusion for patterns: routine 1-3 file reads, following explicit instructions, basic tool use = NOT patterns
- context-gathering threshold: "5+ files, cross-module exploration, or unfamiliar territory"
- `_reasoning` CoT scratchpad field: placed FIRST in JSON schema (forces reasoning before classification), SAVED to DB for prompt tuning
- `[cot-monitor]` console.warn flags models that skip `_reasoning` — remove after ~20 sessions
- Facet-only prompt (backfill) also updated — shares same constants
- Reflect prompts (`reflect-prompts.ts`) NOT impacted — they consume pre-aggregated data
- No explicit "The AI..." prohibition in descriptions — monitoring real output first, will add if needed
- Token budget: +250 input, +190 output per analysis call

## Reflect Feature — Upcoming: Progress Tracking
- Third product layer: Sessions → Patterns → Progress ("Am I getting better?")
- Weekly reflections now shipped (PR #132) — prerequisite complete
- Snapshot-vs-snapshot comparison (not re-aggregation)
- **Friction-to-pattern affinity map ships HERE** (updated to 9-category friction taxonomy, in plan doc)
- Progress filters: `driver = 'user-driven'` = primary growth signal, `collaborative` = secondary, `ai-driven` = informational only
- Progress tracks only `user-actionable` attributed friction (not category membership)
- Hybrid: deterministic delta computation + lightweight LLM narrative (use Haiku — narration not reasoning)
- Manual trigger with missing-week flags in UI
- 3-4 weekly snapshots minimum before offering progress analysis
- Minimum 3 occurrences threshold before including category in deltas
- Full design in `docs/plans/2026-03-09-effective-pattern-normalization.md` (Feature 2 section)

## `llm-expert` Agent Type — Model Issue
- The `llm-expert` subagent_type may fail silently due to "inherit" model config
- Workaround: use `general-purpose` subagent_type with explicit LLM expert instructions in the prompt

## Reflect Feature — Backfill Merges Missing + Outdated (PR #130)
- `reflect backfill` now finds BOTH missing (no facets) AND outdated (stale facets) sessions in one pass
- `/api/facets/outdated` returns `{ count, sessionIds[] }` (was count-only)
- `POST /api/facets/backfill` accepts `force?: boolean` — skips "already has facets" guard
- CLI always sends `force: true`, deduplicates missing+outdated via Set
- Context-aware messaging: "X missing and Y outdated. Processing Z total."

## Completed Phases (Reference)
- Phase 1-4: Foundation through Feature Parity (PRs #49-#63)
- Phase 5: Telemetry (PR #65)
- Phase 6: Polish & Distribution (PR #67, v3.0.0)
- Phase 7: Export & Knowledge Pipeline (PRs #106, #108, v3.5.1-v3.6.0)
- Phase 8: Reflect & Patterns (PRs #109, #110, #113, #116, #118, v3.6.1)
- Phase 8.5: Pattern Normalization (PR #125), Pattern Taxonomy Revision (PR #129), Weekly ISO Week Navigation (PR #132)
- Message Classification: V6 schema (PR #151), Prompt V6 alignment (PR #154)
- LLM Infrastructure: Prompt Caching (PR #180), Cost Tracking V7 schema (PR #181)
- Web repo repurposed (PR #104), export docs (PR #119)

## Prompt V6 Message Classification (PR #154) ✅
- [project_prompt_v6_classification.md](project_prompt_v6_classification.md) — server-side message classification for LLM prompts

## LLM Prompt Caching (PR #180) ✅
- Shared cacheable conversation prefix across session analysis, PQ analysis, and facet extraction
- `ContentBlock` type: `{ type: 'text', text: string, cache_control?: { type: 'ephemeral' } }`
- `LLMMessage.content` now `string | ContentBlock[]` — backward compatible
- `flattenContent()` helper joins blocks to string for non-Anthropic providers
- `SHARED_ANALYST_SYSTEM_PROMPT` (~100 tokens) replaces per-analysis system prompts
- Old system prompt exports kept with `@deprecated` — content moved to instruction builder functions
- `buildCacheableConversationBlock()` wraps conversation with `cache_control` marker
- **Critical design rule**: conversation-only in cached block, project name/metadata in non-cached suffix (otherwise per-session variables break cache hits)
- Anthropic: `anthropic-beta: prompt-caching-2024-07-31` header, `ContentBlock[]` passthrough, cache token parsing
- OpenAI: automatic prefix caching (free, no code changes beyond matching prefix)
- Gemini/Ollama: `flattenContent()` only, no cache support (Gemini deferred — different API)
- `LLMResponse.usage` extended with optional `cacheCreationTokens`, `cacheReadTokens`
- Estimated savings: 32% (Anthropic), 25% (OpenAI) on duplicate conversation input
- Reflect/export prompts NOT impacted (use pre-aggregated data, not raw conversations)

## LLM Analysis Cost Tracking (PR #181) ✅
- Schema V7: `analysis_usage` table — composite PK `(session_id, analysis_type)`, upsert on re-analysis
- `saveAnalysisUsage()` uses ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (changed from INSERT OR REPLACE in PR #247)
- Dashboard: `AnalysisCostLine` with Popover, Ollama shows "local (free)"

## Analysis DB Helper Migration (PR #249) ✅
- Moved `analysis-db.ts` + `analysis-usage-db.ts` from `server/src/llm/` to `cli/src/analysis/`
- Server files are thin re-exports — zero breaking changes
- `COALESCE(excluded.session_message_count, analysis_usage.session_message_count)` pattern preserves CLI-written values during server upserts
- `ANALYSIS_VERSION` as parameter with default (not hardcoded)
- `SaveAnalysisUsageData.session_message_count` is optional — CLI writes it, server passes null

---
name: llama.cpp Provider Integration
description: llamacpp LLM provider + Gemma 4 model support — PR #268, merged 2026-04-03
type: project
---

## llama.cpp Provider + Gemma 4 (PR #268, Issue #267) ✅

New `llamacpp` LLM provider targeting llama-server's OpenAI-compatible API at `/v1/chat/completions`.

**Key design decisions:**
- Separate provider (not reusing OpenAI) — different default URL (8080), no auth, lower temperature, `response_format: { type: "json_object" }`
- Temperature 0.3 (vs 0.7 default) — LLM Expert requirement for quantized models
- `getMaxInputTokens(provider)` in `analysis-internal.ts` — returns 24K for llamacpp, 80K default
- Single retry on JSON parse failure with validation of retry result
- 120s default chat timeout (`AbortSignal.timeout`) for slow local inference
- Model discovery via `GET /v1/models` + dashboard "Discover Loaded Model" button

**Files (22 changed):**
- `server/src/llm/providers/llamacpp.ts` — core provider implementation
- `server/src/llm/analysis-internal.ts` — `getMaxInputTokens()` function
- Token limit applied in: analysis.ts, facet-extraction.ts, prompt-quality-analysis.ts
- Every `=== 'ollama'` check co-located with `'llamacpp'` (full audit)

**Gemma 4 models added to:**
- llamacpp: `gemma-4-12b`, `gemma-4-27b`, `custom`
- Ollama: `gemma4`, `gemma4:27b`
- Gemini: `gemma-3-27b-it` (via Gemini API)

**LLMProvider type:** `'openai' | 'anthropic' | 'gemini' | 'ollama' | 'llamacpp'` (5 providers)

**Tech debt opened:**
1. SettingsPage duplicated provider types (TODO comment added)
2. Simplified prompts for 12B quantized models (deferred)

- Outdated detection: UNION query flags missing `driver` OR missing `category` (combined arm with OR)
- Driver value validation: console.warn for unrecognized values in parseAnalysisResponse
- Dashboard: dominant driver badge (blue=user, violet=AI, green=collaborative)
- Affinity map DEFERRED to Progress PR (dead code with no consumer)

## Reflect Feature — Weekly ISO Week Navigation (PR #132) ✅
- Replaced 7d/30d/90d/all sliding window with ISO week-based navigation (`2026-W10`)
- `parseIsoWeek()` + `formatIsoWeek()` canonical in `server/src/routes/shared-aggregation.ts`
- Dashboard copies (`getCurrentIsoWeek`, `parseIsoWeekBounds`) have "keep in sync" / "adapted from" comments
- New `GET /api/reflect/weeks` endpoint returns last 8 weeks with session counts + snapshot status (2 bulk queries)
- New `WeekSelector` component: arrows, week label, dot indicators, keyboard nav
- CLI: `--period` removed, replaced with `--week <YYYY-WNN>` (default: current ISO week, validated regex + range 1-53 + year 2020-2100)
- `MIN_FACETS_FOR_REFLECT` lowered from 20 → 8 for weekly scope
- Old `7d/30d/90d` snapshot rows orphaned in DB (harmless, no migration needed)
- `reflect_snapshots.period` column is TEXT — accepts any string, no schema change

## Prompt Quality Taxonomy Revision (PR #136) ✅
- 7 deficit categories: `vague-request`, `missing-context`, `late-constraint`, `unclear-correction`, `scope-drift`, `missing-acceptance-criteria`, `assumption-not-surfaced`
- 3 strength categories: `precise-request`, `effective-context`, `productive-correction`
- **Two-layer output**: takeaways (user-facing before/after on Insights page) + findings (categorized for Reflect aggregation)
- **5 dimension scores** (0-100): `context_provision`, `request_specificity`, `scope_management`, `information_timing`, `correction_quality`
- Normalizer at `server/src/llm/prompt-quality-normalize.ts` (mirrors friction/pattern normalizers, 44 aliases)
- Dashboard utils at `dashboard/src/lib/prompt-quality-utils.ts` (labels + strength set, "keep in sync" with server)
- `CategoryBadge` uses finding's `type` prop as truth, falls back to `getPQCategoryType()`
- `bullets` column stores `[]` for new-schema PQ insights — takeaways live in `metadata.takeaways`
- Backward compat: dual-read `efficiency_score ?? efficiencyScore` in 4 dashboard consumers
- Schema detection: `Array.isArray(metadata.findings)` → new schema, else legacy
- `correction_quality` defaults to 75 when no corrections needed (prompt instruction)
- Agentic/delegation session edge case in classification guidance
- `[pq-monitor]` console.warn for missing categories and unexpected type values
- Dormant `antiPatterns` param removed from `generateRulesSkillsPrompt` in reflect-prompts.ts
- `scope-drift` (PQ) ↔ `scope-creep` (friction) cross-taxonomy mapping deferred to Progress PR

## Reflect Feature — Attribution Rewrite (PR #138) ✅
- Actor-neutral friction category definitions (removed "AI..." subject from all 9 categories)
- 3-step CoT attribution decision tree: External? → User-preventable? → AI capability
- "Lean user-actionable when mixed" tiebreaker maintained
- User infrastructure recognition in pattern driver tree (checked FIRST): CLAUDE.md → user request → AI autonomous → collaborative
- Baseline exclusion for patterns: routine 1-3 file reads, following explicit instructions, basic tool use = NOT patterns
- context-gathering threshold: "5+ files, cross-module exploration, or unfamiliar territory"
- `_reasoning` CoT scratchpad field: placed FIRST in JSON schema (forces reasoning before classification), SAVED to DB for prompt tuning
- `[cot-monitor]` console.warn flags models that skip `_reasoning` — remove after ~20 sessions
- Facet-only prompt (backfill) also updated — shares same constants
- Reflect prompts (`reflect-prompts.ts`) NOT impacted — they consume pre-aggregated data
- No explicit "The AI..." prohibition in descriptions — monitoring real output first, will add if needed
- Token budget: +250 input, +190 output per analysis call

## Reflect Feature — Upcoming: Progress Tracking
- Third product layer: Sessions → Patterns → Progress ("Am I getting better?")
- Weekly reflections now shipped (PR #132) — prerequisite complete
- Snapshot-vs-snapshot comparison (not re-aggregation)
- **Friction-to-pattern affinity map ships HERE** (updated to 9-category friction taxonomy, in plan doc)
- Progress filters: `driver = 'user-driven'` = primary growth signal, `collaborative` = secondary, `ai-driven` = informational only
- Progress tracks only `user-actionable` attributed friction (not category membership)
- Hybrid: deterministic delta computation + lightweight LLM narrative (use Haiku — narration not reasoning)
- Manual trigger with missing-week flags in UI
- 3-4 weekly snapshots minimum before offering progress analysis
- Minimum 3 occurrences threshold before including category in deltas
- Full design in `docs/plans/2026-03-09-effective-pattern-normalization.md` (Feature 2 section)

## `llm-expert` Agent Type — Model Issue
- The `llm-expert` subagent_type may fail silently due to "inherit" model config
- Workaround: use `general-purpose` subagent_type with explicit LLM expert instructions in the prompt

## Reflect Feature — Backfill Merges Missing + Outdated (PR #130)
- `reflect backfill` now finds BOTH missing (no facets) AND outdated (stale facets) sessions in one pass
- `/api/facets/outdated` returns `{ count, sessionIds[] }` (was count-only)
- `POST /api/facets/backfill` accepts `force?: boolean` — skips "already has facets" guard
- CLI always sends `force: true`, deduplicates missing+outdated via Set
- Context-aware messaging: "X missing and Y outdated. Processing Z total."

## Completed Phases (Reference)
- Phase 1-4: Foundation through Feature Parity (PRs #49-#63)
- Phase 5: Telemetry (PR #65)
- Phase 6: Polish & Distribution (PR #67, v3.0.0)
- Phase 7: Export & Knowledge Pipeline (PRs #106, #108, v3.5.1-v3.6.0)
- Phase 8: Reflect & Patterns (PRs #109, #110, #113, #116, #118, v3.6.1)
- Phase 8.5: Pattern Normalization (PR #125), Pattern Taxonomy Revision (PR #129), Weekly ISO Week Navigation (PR #132)
- Message Classification: V6 schema (PR #151), Prompt V6 alignment (PR #154)
- LLM Infrastructure: Prompt Caching (PR #180), Cost Tracking V7 schema (PR #181)
- Web repo repurposed (PR #104), export docs (PR #119)

## Prompt V6 Message Classification (PR #154) ✅
- [project_prompt_v6_classification.md](project_prompt_v6_classification.md) — server-side message classification for LLM prompts

## LLM Prompt Caching (PR #180) ✅
- Shared cacheable conversation prefix across session analysis, PQ analysis, and facet extraction
- `ContentBlock` type: `{ type: 'text', text: string, cache_control?: { type: 'ephemeral' } }`
- `LLMMessage.content` now `string | ContentBlock[]` — backward compatible
- `flattenContent()` helper joins blocks to string for non-Anthropic providers
- `SHARED_ANALYST_SYSTEM_PROMPT` (~100 tokens) replaces per-analysis system prompts
- Old system prompt exports kept with `@deprecated` — content moved to instruction builder functions
- `buildCacheableConversationBlock()` wraps conversation with `cache_control` marker
- **Critical design rule**: conversation-only in cached block, project name/metadata in non-cached suffix (otherwise per-session variables break cache hits)
- Anthropic: `anthropic-beta: prompt-caching-2024-07-31` header, `ContentBlock[]` passthrough, cache token parsing
- OpenAI: automatic prefix caching (free, no code changes beyond matching prefix)
- Gemini/Ollama: `flattenContent()` only, no cache support (Gemini deferred — different API)
- `LLMResponse.usage` extended with optional `cacheCreationTokens`, `cacheReadTokens`
- Estimated savings: 32% (Anthropic), 25% (OpenAI) on duplicate conversation input
- Reflect/export prompts NOT impacted (use pre-aggregated data, not raw conversations)

## LLM Analysis Cost Tracking (PR #181) ✅
- Schema V7: `analysis_usage` table — composite PK `(session_id, analysis_type)`, upsert on re-analysis
- `saveAnalysisUsage()` uses ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (changed from INSERT OR REPLACE in PR #247)
- Dashboard: `AnalysisCostLine` with Popover, Ollama shows "local (free)"

## Analysis DB Helper Migration (PR #249) ✅
- Moved `analysis-db.ts` + `analysis-usage-db.ts` from `server/src/llm/` to `cli/src/analysis/`
- Server files are thin re-exports — zero breaking changes
- `COALESCE(excluded.session_message_count, analysis_usage.session_message_count)` pattern preserves CLI-written values during server upserts
- `ANALYSIS_VERSION` as parameter with default (not hardcoded)
- `SaveAnalysisUsageData.session_message_count` is optional — CLI writes it, server passes null

# Code Insights — Auto Memory

## Agent Spawning Constraint (CRITICAL)
- Sub-agents (PM, TA, Dev) CANNOT spawn other agents via the Task tool
- Only the orchestrator (main Claude session) can spawn teammates
- PM must send SPAWN_REQUEST messages to orchestrator when it needs agents
- Fixed in `.claude/commands/start-feature.md` on 2026-02-27

## Product Vision
- Free, open-source, local-first tool helping developers analyze AI coding sessions
- Personal learning tool. No team/org features. No cloud sync. No monetization.
- Multi-source: Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, VS Code Copilot Chat (5 providers)
- Architecture: Single-repo pnpm monorepo (CLI + Dashboard SPA + Hono server)
- Database: SQLite at `~/.code-insights/data.db`, Schema V7 (V8 planned for native analysis)

## Native Analysis Feature (Phase 12, v4.8.0) — IN PROGRESS
- [project_native_analysis_feature.md](project_native_analysis_feature.md) — Zero-config insights via Claude Code hooks

## llama.cpp Provider + Gemma 4 (PR #268) ✅
- [project_llamacpp_provider.md](project_llamacpp_provider.md) — New llamacpp LLM provider, Gemma 4 models, 24K token cap, temp 0.3

## User Preferences (CRITICAL)
- **Always use Subagent-Driven execution** — never ask "which approach?" when executing plans. Default to subagent-driven (this session) over parallel sessions unless the user explicitly requests otherwise.
- **Always use `/start-feature` for big changes** — multi-file features (3+ files, cross-package changes) MUST go through `/start-feature` with full ceremony.
- **NO MVPs, NO PROTOTYPES** — product is LIVE. Always design and implement the FULL solution.
- **Quality bar is HIGH** — no mediocre results. Simple, clean, effective. Don't over-engineer.
- **Ask, don't assume** — when in doubt about an approach or decision, ask the user.
- **Code reviews include LLM expert** — conditional 4th reviewer when PR touches `server/src/llm/`, LLM API calls, structured output schemas, or token budgets.

## Project Conventions
- Dashboard types use snake_case matching SQLite/API responses
- FOUC prevention script in index.html must stay in sync with ThemeProvider
- Radix UI Sheet requires SheetDescription for ARIA compliance

## SQLite Migration Pattern
- `applyV1` = base schema via SCHEMA_SQL, `applyV2+` = incremental changes
- ALWAYS use `INSERT OR IGNORE INTO schema_version` (not bare INSERT) — UNIQUE constraint on version column
- New indexes/schema elements after V1 should live ONLY in their applyVN function (not in SCHEMA_SQL)
- Migration functions must be in chronological order in migrate.ts (applyV2, applyV3, applyV4, applyV5)
- Current version: **V7** (V6: compact_count/auto_compact_count/slash_commands, V7: analysis_usage table)

## Related Repos (Cross-Repo References)
- **code-insights-web** (marketing, docs, blog): `../code-insights-web/`
- **batonship** (separate project): `../../batonship/batonship/`
- All Claude Code sessions for code-insights run from `code-insights/` dir, not web repo.

## Dynamic Review Specialists (PR #227, 2026-03-21)
- [project_dynamic_review_specialists.md](project_dynamic_review_specialists.md) — Static reviewers replaced with dynamic domain specialists + Runtime Verification Rule

## Docs Structure (Updated 2026-03-21)
- CLAUDE.md restructured as quick reference (~250 lines) with links to sub-docs
- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` — repo structure, data flow, providers, SQLite schema, types, API routes, pages
- `docs/AGENTS.md` — agent suite, ceremony, team workflow, code review, delegation
- `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` — branch discipline, hookify, verification, version bump, config, dev notes
- `docs/PRODUCT.md` — product features, source tools, insight categories, export, reflect
- `docs/VISION.md` — philosophy, phase history, non-goals
- `docs/ROADMAP.md` — phase milestones, version table
- `docs/REVIEW-SPECIALISTS.md` — domain specialist registry, selection algorithm, Runtime Verification Rule
- `docs/plans/` — only pending plans (gamification-shareable-badges.md)
- 12 stale plan files deleted (completed PRs #108-#118, launch content)

## Telemetry Model
- PostHog fully integrated in CLI + Dashboard (not "deferred")
- Opt-out model (enabled by default), NOT opt-in
- 14 event types, stable anonymous device ID (SHA-256)
- Respects CODE_INSIGHTS_TELEMETRY_DISABLED and DO_NOT_TRACK env vars

## Hook Enforcement (PR #217)
- [feedback_ci_gate_enforcement.md](feedback_ci_gate_enforcement.md) — PR creation requires build + test pass
- [feedback_hook_patterns.md](feedback_hook_patterns.md) — Hookify rules ARE real hooks, regex anchoring, JSON protocol, zsh compat

## Key Lessons Learned
- Client-side Mermaid in Next.js static sites is unreliable — pre-render diagrams as static images
- Tailwind v4 + shadcn/ui requires: `@custom-variant dark`, `@theme inline`, `@import "tw-animate-css"`
- `@hono/node-server` and `hono` must be in `cli/package.json` deps for npm-installed users
- After merging dashboard/server changes, must run `pnpm build` from repo root

## Reflect Feature — Pattern Normalization (PR #125)
- 8 canonical effective pattern categories: structured-planning, incremental-implementation, verification-workflow, systematic-debugging, self-correction, context-gathering, domain-expertise, effective-tooling
- Pattern normalizer at `server/src/llm/pattern-normalize.ts` (mirrors friction-normalize.ts)
- Normalization at write time in `saveFacetsToDb()` + belt-and-suspenders at read time
- Confidence >= 50 filter for effective patterns at aggregation time in SQL
- Dashboard: category heading + description sub-items (cap 3 + "+N more")
- `category: string` is REQUIRED on EffectivePattern (no backward compat — Reflect not yet released)

- **Attribution field** on friction points: `"user-actionable" | "ai-capability" | "environmental"`

- Contrastive classification guidance with diagnostic questions per category
- Outdated detection: UNION query flags missing `attribution` OR missing `category`
- Dashboard: friction bar chart → category+description list (matches patterns layout)
- Friction-to-Pattern affinity map designed (e.g., stale-assumptions → context-gathering)
- Environmental categories (documentation-gap, tooling-limitation) excluded from Progress tracking

## Completed Phases (Reference)
- Phase 1-4: Foundation through Feature Parity (PRs #49-#63)
- Phase 5: Telemetry (PR #65)
- Phase 6: Polish & Distribution (PR #67, v3.0.0)
- Phase 7: Export & Knowledge Pipeline (PRs #106, #108, v3.5.1-v3.6.0)
- Phase 8: Reflect & Patterns (PRs #109, #110, #113, #116, #118, v3.6.1)
- Phase 8.5: Pattern Normalization (PR #125), Pattern Taxonomy Revision (PR #129), Weekly ISO Week Navigation (PR #132)
- Message Classification: V6 schema (PR #151), Prompt V6 alignment (PR #154)
- LLM Infrastructure: Prompt Caching (PR #180), Cost Tracking V7 schema (PR #181)
- Web repo repurposed (PR #104), export docs (PR #119)

## Prompt V6 Message Classification (PR #154) ✅
- [project_prompt_v6_classification.md](project_prompt_v6_classification.md) — server-side message classification for LLM prompts

## LLM Prompt Caching (PR #180) ✅
- Shared cacheable conversation prefix across session analysis, PQ analysis, and facet extraction
- `ContentBlock` type: `{ type: 'text', text: string, cache_control?: { type: 'ephemeral' } }`
- `LLMMessage.content` now `string | ContentBlock[]` — backward compatible
- `flattenContent()` helper joins blocks to string for non-Anthropic providers
- `SHARED_ANALYST_SYSTEM_PROMPT` (~100 tokens) replaces per-analysis system prompts
- Old system prompt exports kept with `@deprecated` — content moved to instruction builder functions
- `buildCacheableConversationBlock()` wraps conversation with `cache_control` marker
- **Critical design rule**: conversation-only in cached block, project name/metadata in non-cached suffix (otherwise per-session variables break cache hits)
- Anthropic: `anthropic-beta: prompt-caching-2024-07-31` header, `ContentBlock[]` passthrough, cache token parsing
- OpenAI: automatic prefix caching (free, no code changes beyond matching prefix)
- Gemini/Ollama: `flattenContent()` only, no cache support (Gemini deferred — different API)
- `LLMResponse.usage` extended with optional `cacheCreationTokens`, `cacheReadTokens`
- Estimated savings: 32% (Anthropic), 25% (OpenAI) on duplicate conversation input
- Reflect/export prompts NOT impacted (use pre-aggregated data, not raw conversations)

## LLM Analysis Cost Tracking (PR #181) ✅
- Schema V7: `analysis_usage` table — composite PK `(session_id, analysis_type)`, upsert on re-analysis
- `saveAnalysisUsage()` uses ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (changed from INSERT OR REPLACE in PR #247)
- Dashboard: `AnalysisCostLine` with Popover, Ollama shows "local (free)"

## Analysis DB Helper Migration (PR #249) ✅
- Moved `analysis-db.ts` + `analysis-usage-db.ts` from `server/src/llm/` to `cli/src/analysis/`
- Server files are thin re-exports — zero breaking changes
- `COALESCE(excluded.session_message_count, analysis_usage.session_message_count)` pattern preserves CLI-written values during server upserts
- `ANALYSIS_VERSION` as parameter with default (not hardcoded)
- `SaveAnalysisUsageData.session_message_count` is optional — CLI writes it, server passes null

All provider references are concentrated in:

The site emphasizes **local-first, privacy-first design** — all data stays on the user's machine, with optional cloud analysis via user's own API keys.

Got the full picture. Here's the plan for updating the marketing site.

---

## Release Plan: Marketing Site Updates for llama.cpp + Gemma 4

### What Changed in the Product
- New `llamacpp` LLM provider (5th provider)
- Gemma 4 models added to llamacpp, Ollama, and Gemini
- Users can now run `llama-server` directly — no Ollama middleman needed
- Free, fully local inference with Google's latest model

### Files to Update (13 locations across 10 files)

#### Tier 1 — Must Update (provider lists are now wrong)

| # | File | What to Change |
|---|------|----------------|
| 1 | `src/content/docs/reference/configuration.mdx` (lines 38-53) | Add llama.cpp to supported providers list, config commands, base URL docs |
| 2 | `src/content/docs/reference/cli-commands.mdx` (lines 152-166) | Add `llamacpp` to `--provider` values, document `--base-url` default 8080 |
| 3 | `src/content/docs/getting-started/quick-start.mdx` (lines 44-49) | Add "llama.cpp" to provider list alongside Ollama for local inference |
| 4 | `src/content/docs/guides/claude-code-setup.mdx` | Add llama.cpp to "configure any LLM provider" list |
| 5 | `src/content/docs/guides/insights-analysis.mdx` | Mention llama.cpp as a provider option |
| 6 | `src/content/docs/faq.mdx` (lines 28, 95) | Update "Ollama for fully local inference" → "Ollama or llama.cpp for fully local inference" + add Gemma 4 mention |
| 7 | `src/content/docs/getting-started/introduction.mdx` | Update provider count from 4 to 5 if listed |

#### Tier 2 — Marketing Updates (feature promotion)

| # | File | What to Change |
|---|------|----------------|
| 8 | `src/components/marketing/HowItWorksSection.tsx` | Step 3: add "llama.cpp" to provider list ("OpenAI, Gemini, Ollama, llama.cpp") |
| 9 | `src/app/page.tsx` (structured data) | Add llama.cpp to JSON-LD supported software list |
| 10 | `src/components/marketing/PrivacySection.tsx` | Strengthen local-first pitch — "run Gemma 4 locally via llama.cpp or Ollama" |

#### Tier 3 — New Content

| # | Type | What to Create |
|---|------|----------------|
| 11 | New doc: `guides/local-llm-setup.mdx` | Dedicated guide for local LLM setup — covers both Ollama AND llama.cpp with Gemma 4. Installation, model download, `llama-server` startup, configuration, troubleshooting. This is the highest-value new content. |
| 12 | Blog post: `blog/local-ai-analysis-gemma4.mdx` | "Free AI Session Analysis with Gemma 4 + llama.cpp" — SEO play for "local LLM", "free AI coding tools", "Gemma 4" keywords |
| 13 | Nav update: `src/lib/docs/docs-nav.ts` | Add the new guide to the sidebar navigation under Guides |

### What NOT to Change
- **Screenshots** — Settings page screenshots (`settings-light.png`, `settings-dark.png`) ideally should show the new llamacpp provider, but this requires a running dashboard. Flag as follow-up, don't block release.
- **Architecture diagram** — llama.cpp is another provider behind the same abstraction. No architectural change to diagram.
- **Multi-source section** — This covers source tools (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.), not LLM providers. No change needed.

### Content Strategy for the New Guide

**`guides/local-llm-setup.mdx`** should cover:
1. **Why local** — free, private, no API keys, offline-capable
2. **Option A: Ollama** (simpler) — `ollama pull gemma4` → `code-insights config llm --provider ollama --model gemma4`
3. **Option B: llama.cpp** (more control) — download GGUF, `llama-server -m model.gguf`, configure
4. **Recommended models** — Gemma 4 12B Q4_K_M (8GB VRAM), Gemma 4 27B Q4_K_M (16GB VRAM), Qwen3 14B
5. **Hardware requirements** — VRAM table by model/quantization
6. **Troubleshooting** — connection refused, slow inference, JSON errors

### Pros/Cons of this Approach

| Pro | Con |
|-----|-----|
| Consistent messaging — every provider list updated | 13 changes across 2 repos, need to coordinate |
| SEO value — "local LLM", "Gemma 4", "llama.cpp" are trending search terms | Blog post requires effort beyond code changes |
| Dedicated local setup guide reduces support burden | Guide needs hardware requirement research for accuracy |
| Strengthens local-first positioning (key differentiator) | Screenshots are stale until manually updated |

---

Want me to implement this, or do you want to adjust the scope first?

file.content:
Shows sync paths, dashboard port, LLM provider, and telemetry status.

## LLM settings

LLM provider settings (provider, model, API key, base URL) are stored in `config.json` under the `dashboard.llm` key. Configure via CLI or the dashboard Settings page:

```bash
# Interactive wizard
code-insights config llm

# Non-interactive
code-insights config llm --provider openai --model gpt-4o --api-key sk-...

# View current config
code-insights config llm --show
```

Supported providers: **OpenAI**, **Anthropic**, **Google Gemini**, **Ollama** (local, no API key needed).

## Telemetry

Anonymous, opt-out telemetry collects command name, success/failure, CLI version, OS, and session count bucket. No filenames, project names, session content, API keys, or personal info is ever collected. Telemetry is **enabled by default** — to disable it immediately: `code-insights telemetry opt-out`, or set `DO_NOT_TRACK=1` / `CODE_INSIGHTS_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1` in your environment.

```bash
code-insights telemetry status     # View what's collected
code-insights telemetry opt-out    # Disable
code-insights telemetry opt-in     # Re-enable
code-insights config set telemetry false  # Alternative
```

Environment variable overrides: `CODE_INSIGHTS_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1` or `DO_NOT_TRACK=1`.

## Resetting

To delete all local data:

```bash
code-insights reset --confirm
```

This deletes `data.db`, `sync-state.json`, and `device-id`. The `config.json` is preserved.

To delete everything including config:

```bash
rm -rf ~/.code-insights
```

file.content:
## Is my data sent to the cloud?

**No.** Code Insights is fully local-first. All session data is stored in a SQLite database on your machine at `~/.code-insights/data.db`. There are no accounts, no cloud services, and no data ever leaves your device.

The only optional network activity is if you configure LLM-powered insights — in that case, session content is sent to your chosen LLM provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, or Ollama for fully local inference).

## How do I install Code Insights?

```bash
npm install -g @code-insights/cli

file.content:
## Can I use Code Insights without an LLM API key?

Yes. LLM analysis is optional. Without an API key, you still get full session syncing, browsing, search, cost tracking, terminal analytics, and the dashboard. The only feature that requires an LLM is the "Analyze" button for extracting insights.

For fully local LLM inference, you can use [Ollama](https://ollama.com) — no API key or cloud service needed.

## Where is my data stored?

All data lives in `~/.code-insights/`:

45	/** Flat list of all nav items in order — used for prev/next computation and static generation */
46	export const flatNav: NavItem[] = docsNav.flatMap((group) => group.items);
47	
48	/** Returns the previous and next page relative to the given slug */
49	export function getPrevNext(slug: string): { prev: NavItem | null; next: NavItem | null } {
50	  const index = flatNav.findIndex((item) => item.slug === slug);
51	  return {
52	    prev: index > 0 ? flatNav[index - 1] : null,
53	    next: index < flatNav.length - 1 ? flatNav[index + 1] : null,
54	  };
55	}
56	
57	/** Returns the NavGroup that contains the given slug, or null */
58	export function getActiveGroup(slug: string): NavGroup | null {
59	  return docsNav.find((group) => group.items.some((item) => item.slug === slug)) ?? null;
60	}

---
title: "Configuration"
description: "All Code Insights configuration files, data paths, and local storage locations. Covers config.json, sync state, device ID, and the SQLite database."
---

## File locations

All Code Insights data lives in `~/.code-insights/`:

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `~/.code-insights/data.db` | SQLite database (sessions, insights, messages) |
| `~/.code-insights/config.json` | CLI configuration |
| `~/.code-insights/sync-state.json` | Incremental sync state (file modification times) |
| `~/.code-insights/device-id` | Stable device identifier |

## config.json

Created by `code-insights init`. Example:

```json
{
  "sourcePaths": {
    "claudeCode": "/Users/you/.claude/projects",
    "cursor": "/Users/you/Library/Application Support/Cursor/User/globalStorage"
  }
}
```

## Viewing config

```bash
code-insights config
```

Shows sync paths, dashboard port, LLM provider, and telemetry status.

## LLM settings

LLM provider settings (provider, model, API key, base URL) are stored in `config.json` under the `dashboard.llm` key. Configure via CLI or the dashboard Settings page:

```bash
# Interactive wizard
code-insights config llm

# Non-interactive
code-insights config llm --provider openai --model gpt-4o --api-key sk-...

# View current config
code-insights config llm --show
```

Supported providers: **OpenAI**, **Anthropic**, **Google Gemini**, **Ollama** (local, no API key needed).

## Telemetry

Anonymous, opt-out telemetry collects command name, success/failure, CLI version, OS, and session count bucket. No filenames, project names, session content, API keys, or personal info is ever collected. Telemetry is **enabled by default** — to disable it immediately: `code-insights telemetry opt-out`, or set `DO_NOT_TRACK=1` / `CODE_INSIGHTS_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1` in your environment.

```bash
code-insights telemetry status     # View what's collected
code-insights telemetry opt-out    # Disable
code-insights telemetry opt-in     # Re-enable
code-insights config set telemetry false  # Alternative
```

Environment variable overrides: `CODE_INSIGHTS_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1` or `DO_NOT_TRACK=1`.

## Resetting

To delete all local data:

```bash
code-insights reset --confirm
```

This deletes `data.db`, `sync-state.json`, and `device-id`. The `config.json` is preserved.

To delete everything including config:

```bash
rm -rf ~/.code-insights
```

+> **New in v4.8.5:** llama.cpp support lets you run analysis with any GGUF model — including Google's Gemma 4 — directly via `llama-server`, without needing Ollama. See the [Local LLM Setup](/docs/guides/local-llm-setup) guide.

---
title: "CLI Commands"
description: "Complete reference for all code-insights CLI commands and flags — init, sync, stats, dashboard, config, reset, install-hook, and more."
---

## Global

```bash
code-insights --version    # Print version
code-insights --help       # Show help
```

## init

```bash
code-insights init
```

Interactive setup. Detects installed AI coding tools and initializes the local database at `~/.code-insights/data.db`.

## sync

```bash
code-insights sync [options]
```

Syncs sessions from all supported source tools to the local SQLite database.

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--force` | Re-sync all sessions (ignores modification times) |
| `--dry-run` | Preview what would be synced without writing |
| `-q, --quiet` | Suppress all output |
| `--source <tool>` | Sync only from: `claude-code`, `cursor`, `codex-cli`, `copilot-cli`, `copilot` |

### sync prune

```bash
code-insights sync prune
```

Soft-deletes sessions with 2 or fewer messages (trivial abandoned sessions). Shows a preview list and asks for confirmation before making any changes. Deleted sessions are restorable with `sync --force`.

## open

```bash
code-insights open [options]
```

Opens the local dashboard in your default browser. If the dashboard server is already running, this opens a new browser tab to it.

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--project` | Open filtered to the current project |

## dashboard

```bash
code-insights dashboard [options]
```

Starts the embedded Hono server and opens `localhost:7890` in your browser.

| Flag | Default | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `-p, --port <number>` | `7890` | Port number for the dashboard server |
| `--no-open` | — | Do not open browser automatically |

## status

```bash
code-insights status
```

Shows sync statistics: total sessions, messages, last sync time, and source tool breakdown.

## stats

```bash
code-insights stats [subcommand] [options]
```

Terminal analytics. See [Terminal Analytics](/docs/guides/terminal-analytics) for details.

| Subcommand | Description |
|------------|-------------|
| *(none)* | Overview dashboard |
| `cost` | Cost breakdown by project and model |
| `projects` | Per-project detail cards |
| `today` | Today's sessions |
| `models` | Model usage distribution |
| `patterns` | Cross-session patterns summary |

**Shared flags:**

| Flag | Default | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `--period 7d\|30d\|90d\|all` | `7d` | Time range |
| `--project <name>` | — | Scope to a specific project |
| `--source <tool>` | — | Filter by source tool |
| `--no-sync` | — | Skip auto-sync before showing stats |

## reflect

```bash
code-insights reflect [options]
```

Generates cross-session LLM synthesis for the specified ISO week: friction patterns, effective workflows, and CLAUDE.md rule suggestions. Requires the dashboard server to be running (`code-insights dashboard`) and an LLM provider configured.

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--week <YYYY-WNN>` | ISO week to reflect on (e.g., `2026-W10`), defaults to the current week |
| `--section <name>` | Generate a specific section only: `friction-wins`, `rules-skills`, `working-style` |
| `--project <name>` | Scope to a single project |

### reflect backfill

```bash
code-insights reflect backfill [options]
```

Extracts friction and effective-pattern facets for sessions that have insights but are missing pattern data (or have outdated analysis). Shows a count preview and asks for confirmation before making LLM calls. Note: backfill uses `--period` (a rolling age range to target sessions by recency) rather than `--week`, because it operates on session analysis age rather than synthesis scope.

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `-p, --period <period>` | Time range: `7d`, `30d`, `90d`, `all` (default: `all`) |
| `--project <name>` | Scope to a single project |
| `--dry-run` | Show count without backfilling |
| `--prompt-quality` | Run prompt quality analysis instead of facet extraction |

## config

```bash
code-insights config
```

Shows the current configuration summary (sync paths, dashboard port, LLM provider, telemetry status).

### config set

```bash
code-insights config set <key> <value>
```

Set a configuration value. Currently supported keys:

| Key | Values | Description |
|-----|--------|-------------|
| `telemetry` | `true` / `false` | Enable or disable anonymous telemetry |

### config llm

```bash
code-insights config llm [options]
```

Configure the LLM provider for AI-powered session analysis. Without flags, runs an interactive wizard.

| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `--provider <provider>` | LLM provider (`openai`, `anthropic`, `gemini`, `ollama`) |
| `--model <model>` | Model ID (e.g., `gpt-4o`, `claude-sonnet-4-20250514`) |
| `--api-key <key>` | API key for the selected provider |
| `--base-url <url>` | Custom base URL (for Ollama or local endpoints) |
| `--show` | Show current LLM configuration |

## install-hook

```bash
code-insights install-hook
```

Adds a `Stop` hook to `~/.claude/settings.json` that runs `code-insights sync -q` after every Claude Code session.

## uninstall-hook

```bash
code-insights uninstall-hook
```

Removes the auto-sync hook from `~/.claude/settings.json`.

## reset

```bash
code-insights reset --confirm
```

Deletes all local data: `~/.code-insights/data.db`, `sync-state.json`, and `device-id`. Requires the `--confirm` flag to prevent accidental deletion.

## telemetry

```bash
code-insights telemetry [subcommand]
```

View and manage anonymous telemetry settings.

| Subcommand | Description |
|------------|-------------|
| `status` | Show current telemetry status and what data is collected |
| `opt-out` | Disable anonymous telemetry |
| `opt-in` | Re-enable anonymous telemetry |

Telemetry can also be disabled via environment variables: `CODE_INSIGHTS_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1` or `DO_NOT_TRACK=1`.

-| `--base-url <url>` | Custom base URL (for Ollama or local endpoints) |

+| `--base-url <url>` | Custom base URL (Ollama default: `localhost:11434`, llama.cpp default: `localhost:8080`) |

---
title: "Quick Start"
description: "Get Code Insights running in 30 seconds. One command syncs your AI coding sessions and opens the local dashboard. No accounts or API keys required."
---

## Try it instantly

```bash
npx @code-insights/cli
```

No installation needed — try it right now.

## Or install globally

```bash
npm install -g @code-insights/cli
```

Then run a single command to sync your sessions and open the dashboard:

```bash
code-insights
```

That's it. Code Insights auto-detects your AI coding tools (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, VS Code Copilot Chat), syncs all sessions into a local SQLite database, and opens the dashboard at `localhost:7890`.

<Callout type="note">
The first run processes your entire session history and may take a minute. Subsequent runs are incremental.
</Callout>

## Set up auto-sync (optional)

Install a hook that runs a quiet sync whenever a Claude Code session ends — so your dashboard is always up to date without thinking about it:

```bash
code-insights install-hook
```

## Get AI insights

**Claude Code users get zero-config analysis** — the hook you just installed analyzes every session automatically using your Claude subscription. No API key needed. See [Claude Code Setup](/docs/guides/claude-code-setup) for the full guide.

**Using other tools?** Configure an LLM provider for on-demand analysis:

1. In the dashboard, click **Settings** (gear icon)
2. Select a provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, or Ollama for fully local inference)
3. Enter your API key
4. Return to any session and click **Analyze**

## Individual commands reference

These commands are available if you want to run steps separately:

```bash
code-insights init          # Customize settings (optional — runs automatically on first use)
code-insights sync          # Sync sessions without opening the dashboard
code-insights dashboard     # Start the dashboard server (auto-syncs first)
```

## What's next

- [The Dashboard](/docs/guides/dashboard) — Tour of the embedded dashboard
- [Terminal Analytics](/docs/guides/terminal-analytics) — Stats in your terminal without the dashboard
- [Insights & Analysis](/docs/guides/insights-analysis) — Deep dive into LLM-powered insights
- [CLI Commands](/docs/reference/cli-commands) — Every command and flag

---
title: "Claude Code Setup"
description: "Zero-config session analysis for Claude Code users. Install a hook once — every session gets analyzed automatically using your Claude subscription."
---

## Install Code Insights

If you haven't already, install and run Code Insights to sync your sessions:

```bash
npx @code-insights/cli
```

This syncs all your Claude Code sessions and opens the dashboard at `http://localhost:7890`.

## Install the Session Hook

Run this once to enable automatic analysis:

```bash
code-insights install-hook
```

This installs two Claude Code hooks:

- **Stop hook** — syncs the session to your local database when it ends
- **SessionEnd hook** — analyzes the session using your Claude subscription via `claude -p`

No API key needed. No configuration. The analysis uses the same Claude subscription that powers your Claude Code sessions.

### Hook options

```bash
# Install both hooks (default)
code-insights install-hook

# Install only the sync hook (no analysis)
code-insights install-hook --sync-only

# Install only the analysis hook
code-insights install-hook --analysis-only
```

## Verify It Works

1. Start a Claude Code session and do some work
2. End the session (type `/exit` or close the terminal)
3. Run: `code-insights dashboard`
4. Open the session — you should see analysis results: a summary, decisions, learnings, and prompt quality scores

The analysis typically takes 30-60 seconds after the session ends. If you open the dashboard immediately, the session may appear without analysis — refresh after a minute.

## Next Steps

### Backfill existing sessions

If you have sessions that were synced before installing the hook, you can analyze them in bulk:

```bash
# Check how many unanalyzed sessions exist
code-insights insights check

# Analyze all unanalyzed sessions (last 7 days)
code-insights insights check --analyze

# Custom lookback window
code-insights insights check --days 30
```

### Analyze a specific session manually

```bash
# Using your Claude subscription (native mode)
code-insights insights <session_id> --native

# Using a configured LLM provider
code-insights insights <session_id>
```

### Explore weekly reflections

Once you have several analyzed sessions, try cross-session synthesis:

```bash
code-insights reflect
```

### Configure a different LLM provider

If you prefer to use a different model or want analysis for non-Claude-Code sessions, configure any LLM provider:

```bash
code-insights config llm
```

Supported providers: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, and Ollama (free, local). See [Insights & Analysis](/docs/guides/insights-analysis) for details on what each insight type contains.

## Troubleshooting

### Hook didn't fire

Verify the hook is installed:

```bash
cat ~/.claude/settings.json | grep -A2 SessionEnd
```

You should see an entry containing `code-insights insights --hook --native -q`. If not, re-run `code-insights install-hook`.

### Session shows but no analysis

Check that the `claude` CLI is available on your PATH:

```bash
which claude
```

The native runner calls `claude -p` as a subprocess. If Claude Code is installed but not on your PATH, the analysis hook will fail silently.

### Want to use an API key instead?

If you prefer API-key-based analysis (or use Cursor, Codex CLI, or Copilot), configure a provider:

```bash
code-insights config llm
```

See the [Configuration reference](/docs/reference/configuration) for all provider options.

---
title: "FAQ"
description: "Frequently asked questions about Code Insights — installation, privacy, supported tools, costs, and troubleshooting."
---

Code Insights is a free, open-source CLI tool that parses AI coding sessions from multiple tools into a local knowledge base. Here are the most common questions.

## What is Code Insights?

Code Insights is an open-source CLI and embedded dashboard that analyzes your AI coding sessions. It parses session history from Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, and VS Code Copilot Chat, stores everything in a local SQLite database, and provides terminal analytics plus a browser-based dashboard with optional LLM-powered insights.

## Which AI coding tools does Code Insights support?

Code Insights supports **five tools** out of the box:

- **Claude Code** — parses JSONL session files from `~/.claude/projects/`
- **Cursor** — reads the SQLite state database (`state.vscdb`)
- **Codex CLI** — parses rollout JSONL files from `~/.codex/sessions/`
- **Copilot CLI** — reads event JSONL files from `~/.copilot/session-state/`
- **VS Code Copilot Chat** — reads chat session data from VS Code's storage

All supported tools are auto-detected. No per-tool configuration needed. See the [Multi-Source Setup](/docs/guides/multi-source) guide for platform-specific details.

## Is my data sent to the cloud?

**No.** Code Insights is fully local-first. All session data is stored in a SQLite database on your machine at `~/.code-insights/data.db`. There are no accounts, no cloud services, and no data ever leaves your device.

The only optional network activity is if you configure LLM-powered insights — in that case, session content is sent to your chosen LLM provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, or Ollama for fully local inference).

## How do I install Code Insights?

```bash
npm install -g @code-insights/cli
code-insights
```

That's it. Running `code-insights` syncs your sessions and opens the dashboard automatically. No configuration files, API keys, or cloud accounts needed. See the [Installation](/docs/getting-started/installation) and [Quick Start](/docs/getting-started/quick-start) guides for details.

## Is Code Insights free?

Yes. Code Insights is free and open-source under the [MIT license](https://github.com/melagiri/code-insights/blob/master/LICENSE). The entire stack — CLI, embedded dashboard, and Hono server — is available on [GitHub](https://github.com/melagiri/code-insights).

## How does Code Insights track AI coding costs?

The `code-insights stats cost` command shows cost breakdowns by project and model. Cost data is calculated from token usage in your session files. You can filter by time period (`--period 7d|30d|90d|all`), project, or source tool.

The dashboard also shows cost analytics on the Analytics page with visual charts. See [Terminal Analytics](/docs/guides/terminal-analytics) for all stats subcommands.

## What insights can Code Insights extract?

When you run analysis on a session, Code Insights uses your configured LLM to extract structured insights across several types:

- **Decisions** — architectural and design choices made during the session
- **Learnings** — new knowledge gained or patterns discovered
- **Techniques** — specific coding approaches or tool usage patterns
- **Summaries** — concise overviews of what happened in the session
- **Prompt Quality** — five-dimension scores (0–100) with deficit and strength findings
- **Friction points** — per-session friction categorized across 9 types (e.g., `knowledge-gap`, `stale-assumptions`, `scope-creep`) with attribution (`user-actionable`, `ai-capability`, or `environmental`)
- **Effective patterns** — per-session effective behaviors categorized across 8 types (e.g., `structured-planning`, `systematic-debugging`) with driver attribution
- **Facets** — extracted per-session metadata (outcome satisfaction, iteration count, course corrections) used by the Reflect pipeline for cross-session synthesis

Insights are stored locally and searchable in the dashboard. See [Insights & Analysis](/docs/guides/insights-analysis) and the [Reflect & Patterns guide](/docs/guides/reflect-patterns) for details.

## How do I use Reflect to get cross-session patterns?

Run `code-insights reflect` to generate a weekly synthesis of your friction points, effective patterns, and CLAUDE.md rule suggestions. The default is the current ISO week:

```bash
code-insights reflect                    # current week
code-insights reflect --week 2026-W10   # specific week
```

Reflect requires the dashboard server to be running (`code-insights dashboard`) and an LLM configured in Settings. If your sessions don't have facets yet, run `code-insights reflect backfill` first to extract them. See the [Reflect & Patterns guide](/docs/guides/reflect-patterns) for the full workflow.

## How do I sync sessions from Cursor?

Cursor sessions are auto-detected. Run `code-insights sync` and the CLI will find Cursor's `state.vscdb` database in the platform-specific location (macOS, Linux, or Windows). To sync only Cursor sessions, use `code-insights sync --source cursor`.

## How do I sync sessions from Claude Code?

Claude Code sessions are auto-detected from `~/.claude/projects/`. Run `code-insights sync` to process all session files. Use `code-insights sync --source claude-code` to sync only Claude Code sessions.

## How do I sync sessions from VS Code Copilot Chat?

VS Code Copilot Chat sessions are auto-detected from VS Code's `workspaceStorage/` directory. Run `code-insights sync` and the CLI finds chat session files automatically. Use `code-insights sync --source copilot` to sync only VS Code Copilot Chat sessions.

## How do I export my insights?

There are two ways to export. For a single session, open the session detail panel and click the download icon to export as Markdown, Obsidian, or Notion format. For AI-powered synthesis across sessions, open the **Export** page — it uses your configured LLM to compile deduplicated, prioritized knowledge in 4 formats (Agent Rules, Knowledge Brief, Obsidian, Notion) at 3 depth levels. See [Analytics & Export](/docs/guides/analytics-export) for details.

## Can I use Code Insights without an LLM API key?

Yes. LLM analysis is optional. Without an API key, you still get full session syncing, browsing, search, cost tracking, terminal analytics, and the dashboard. The only feature that requires an LLM is the "Analyze" button for extracting insights.

For fully local LLM inference, you can use [Ollama](https://ollama.com) — no API key or cloud service needed.

## Where is my data stored?

All data lives in `~/.code-insights/`:

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `data.db` | SQLite database with all sessions, messages, and insights |
| `config.json` | Configuration (source tools, preferences) |
| `sync-state.json` | Tracks which files have been synced |
| `device-id` | Anonymous device identifier |

To delete everything: `code-insights reset --confirm` or `rm -rf ~/.code-insights`.

---
title: "FAQ"
description: "Frequently asked questions about Code Insights — installation, privacy, supported tools, costs, and troubleshooting."
---

Code Insights is a free, open-source CLI tool that parses AI coding sessions from multiple tools into a local knowledge base. Here are the most common questions.

## What is Code Insights?

Code Insights is an open-source CLI and embedded dashboard that analyzes your AI coding sessions. It parses session history from Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, and VS Code Copilot Chat, stores everything in a local SQLite database, and provides terminal analytics plus a browser-based dashboard with optional LLM-powered insights.

## Which AI coding tools does Code Insights support?

Code Insights supports **five tools** out of the box:

- **Claude Code** — parses JSONL session files from `~/.claude/projects/`
- **Cursor** — reads the SQLite state database (`state.vscdb`)
- **Codex CLI** — parses rollout JSONL files from `~/.codex/sessions/`
- **Copilot CLI** — reads event JSONL files from `~/.copilot/session-state/`
- **VS Code Copilot Chat** — reads chat session data from VS Code's storage

All supported tools are auto-detected. No per-tool configuration needed. See the [Multi-Source Setup](/docs/guides/multi-source) guide for platform-specific details.

## Is my data sent to the cloud?

**No.** Code Insights is fully local-first. All session data is stored in a SQLite database on your machine at `~/.code-insights/data.db`. There are no accounts, no cloud services, and no data ever leaves your device.

The only optional network activity is if you configure LLM-powered insights — in that case, session content is sent to your chosen LLM provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, or Ollama/llama.cpp for fully local inference).

## How do I install Code Insights?

```bash
npm install -g @code-insights/cli
code-insights
```

That's it. Running `code-insights` syncs your sessions and opens the dashboard automatically. No configuration files, API keys, or cloud accounts needed. See the [Installation](/docs/getting-started/installation) and [Quick Start](/docs/getting-started/quick-start) guides for details.

## Is Code Insights free?

Yes. Code Insights is free and open-source under the [MIT license](https://github.com/melagiri/code-insights/blob/master/LICENSE). The entire stack — CLI, embedded dashboard, and Hono server — is available on [GitHub](https://github.com/melagiri/code-insights).

## How does Code Insights track AI coding costs?

The `code-insights stats cost` command shows cost breakdowns by project and model. Cost data is calculated from token usage in your session files. You can filter by time period (`--period 7d|30d|90d|all`), project, or source tool.

The dashboard also shows cost analytics on the Analytics page with visual charts. See [Terminal Analytics](/docs/guides/terminal-analytics) for all stats subcommands.

## What insights can Code Insights extract?

When you run analysis on a session, Code Insights uses your configured LLM to extract structured insights across several types:

- **Decisions** — architectural and design choices made during the session
- **Learnings** — new knowledge gained or patterns discovered
- **Techniques** — specific coding approaches or tool usage patterns
- **Summaries** — concise overviews of what happened in the session
- **Prompt Quality** — five-dimension scores (0–100) with deficit and strength findings
- **Friction points** — per-session friction categorized across 9 types (e.g., `knowledge-gap`, `stale-assumptions`, `scope-creep`) with attribution (`user-actionable`, `ai-capability`, or `environmental`)
- **Effective patterns** — per-session effective behaviors categorized across 8 types (e.g., `structured-planning`, `systematic-debugging`) with driver attribution
- **Facets** — extracted per-session metadata (outcome satisfaction, iteration count, course corrections) used by the Reflect pipeline for cross-session synthesis

Insights are stored locally and searchable in the dashboard. See [Insights & Analysis](/docs/guides/insights-analysis) and the [Reflect & Patterns guide](/docs/guides/reflect-patterns) for details.

## How do I use Reflect to get cross-session patterns?

Run `code-insights reflect` to generate a weekly synthesis of your friction points, effective patterns, and CLAUDE.md rule suggestions. The default is the current ISO week:

```bash
code-insights reflect                    # current week
code-insights reflect --week 2026-W10   # specific week
```

Reflect requires the dashboard server to be running (`code-insights dashboard`) and an LLM configured in Settings. If your sessions don't have facets yet, run `code-insights reflect backfill` first to extract them. See the [Reflect & Patterns guide](/docs/guides/reflect-patterns) for the full workflow.

## How do I sync sessions from Cursor?

Cursor sessions are auto-detected. Run `code-insights sync` and the CLI will find Cursor's `state.vscdb` database in the platform-specific location (macOS, Linux, or Windows). To sync only Cursor sessions, use `code-insights sync --source cursor`.

## How do I sync sessions from Claude Code?

Claude Code sessions are auto-detected from `~/.claude/projects/`. Run `code-insights sync` to process all session files. Use `code-insights sync --source claude-code` to sync only Claude Code sessions.

## How do I sync sessions from VS Code Copilot Chat?

VS Code Copilot Chat sessions are auto-detected from VS Code's `workspaceStorage/` directory. Run `code-insights sync` and the CLI finds chat session files automatically. Use `code-insights sync --source copilot` to sync only VS Code Copilot Chat sessions.

## How do I export my insights?

There are two ways to export. For a single session, open the session detail panel and click the download icon to export as Markdown, Obsidian, or Notion format. For AI-powered synthesis across sessions, open the **Export** page — it uses your configured LLM to compile deduplicated, prioritized knowledge in 4 formats (Agent Rules, Knowledge Brief, Obsidian, Notion) at 3 depth levels. See [Analytics & Export](/docs/guides/analytics-export) for details.

## Can I use Code Insights without an LLM API key?

Yes. LLM analysis is optional. Without an API key, you still get full session syncing, browsing, search, cost tracking, terminal analytics, and the dashboard. The only feature that requires an LLM is the "Analyze" button for extracting insights.

For fully local LLM inference, you can use [Ollama](https://ollama.com) — no API key or cloud service needed.

## Where is my data stored?

All data lives in `~/.code-insights/`:

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `data.db` | SQLite database with all sessions, messages, and insights |
| `config.json` | Configuration (source tools, preferences) |
| `sync-state.json` | Tracks which files have been synced |
| `device-id` | Anonymous device identifier |

To delete everything: `code-insights reset --confirm` or `rm -rf ~/.code-insights`.

+For fully local LLM inference, you can use [Ollama](https://ollama.com) or [llama.cpp](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp) — no API key or cloud service needed. We recommend Gemma 4 12B as a strong local model. See the [Local LLM Setup](/docs/guides/local-llm-setup) guide.

toolUseResult.originalFile: --- title: "Insights & Analysis" description: "Use LLM-powered analysis to extract decisions, learnings, techniques, and patterns from your AI coding sessions. Supports OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, and Ollama." --- ## What are Code Insights insights? When you analyze a session, the CLI server sends the conversation to your configured LLM provider and extracts structured insights. These are stored in the local SQLite database and displayed in the dashboard. The analysis uses a "senior staff engineer writing for a knowledge base" persona — each insight is written as if a future developer will read it 6 months from now with no context about the session. ## What types of insights are extracted? | Type | What it captures | |------|-----------------| | **Summary** | Narrative of what was accomplished, outcome status (success/partial/abandoned/blocked), and key artifacts changed | | **Decision** | A technical choice with full context: situation, reasoning, rejected alternatives, trade-offs, and conditions for revisiting | | **Learning** | A technical discovery: observable symptom, root cause, transferable takeaway, and when it applies | | **Technique** | A reusable approach or pattern demonstrated | | **Prompt Quality** | Five dimension scores (0–100), deficit and strength findings, and before/after takeaways | ### Decision structure Each decision insight includes: - **Situation** — what problem or requirement led to the decision - **Choice** — what was chosen and how it was implemented - **Reasoning** — the key factors that tipped the decision - **Alternatives** — rejected options with reasons why - **Trade-offs** — what downsides were accepted - **Revisit when** — conditions under which to reconsider ### Learning structure Each learning insight includes: - **Symptom** — what went wrong or was confusing - **Root cause** — the underlying technical reason - **Takeaway** — the transferable lesson for similar situations - **Applies when** — conditions under which this knowledge is relevant ## How do I run analysis on a session? 1. Open the dashboard: `code-insights dashboard` 2. Navigate to **Sessions** 3. Click into any session 4. Click **Analyze** (requires LLM configured in Settings) Analysis runs server-side via the Hono API. Progress is streamed back to the dashboard in real time via Server-Sent Events (SSE), so you can see each insight as it's extracted. Your API key stays on your machine and is never sent anywhere else. During analysis, the LLM also classifies the session into one of 7 character types (deep_focus, bug_hunt, feature_build, etc.) and writes it back to the session record. ## How do I analyze multiple sessions at once? From the **Dashboard** page, click **Analyze** on the unanalyzed sessions card to run analysis across all pending sessions. You can also analyze individual sessions from the session detail page. Progress is shown in real time via SSE streaming. ## How does prompt quality scoring work? The prompt quality analysis evaluates your prompting across five dimensions, each scored 0–100. ### Dimension scores | Dimension | What it measures | |-----------|-----------------| | `context_provision` | How well you provided upfront context before making requests | | `request_specificity` | How precisely and specifically you stated what you needed | | `scope_management` | How well you kept requests focused without drifting | | `information_timing` | Whether key constraints were given early vs. revealed late | | `correction_quality` | How effectively you guided the AI when corrections were needed | ### Deficit categories These categories flag problems in your prompting behavior: | Category | What it flags | |----------|--------------| | `vague-request` | Request lacked enough specificity for the AI to act correctly | | `missing-context` | Necessary background or file context was absent | | `late-constraint` | Key requirements were revealed after work had already started | | `unclear-correction` | A correction redirected the AI but didn't explain what was wrong or what to do instead | | `scope-drift` | The session covered too many unrelated objectives | | `missing-acceptance-criteria` | No clear definition of "done" was given | | `assumption-not-surfaced` | An implicit expectation wasn't stated, causing misalignment | ### Strength categories These categories flag effective prompting behavior worth repeating: | Category | What it means | |----------|--------------| | `precise-request` | Request was specific and actionable from the start | | `effective-context` | Relevant background and constraints were provided upfront | | `productive-correction` | Corrections were clear and led directly to better output | ### Two-layer output Each prompt quality analysis produces: - **Takeaways** — user-facing before/after examples shown on the Insights page, one per significant finding - **Findings** — categorized items (deficit or strength) used for Reflect aggregation across sessions The Reflect pipeline aggregates findings across weeks to show recurring prompting patterns over time. ## LLM analysis cost tracking Every analysis call (session insights, prompt quality, facet extraction) records its token usage and estimated cost in the `analysis_usage` table. This lets the dashboard show you exactly what each analysis cost — broken down by analysis type, model, and session. The cost display appears in the session detail panel next to each analysis result. Costs are estimates based on public provider pricing and may differ slightly from your actual bill. ## Where are insights stored? All insights are stored in the `insights` table in `~/.code-insights/data.db`. You can export individual sessions as Markdown from the session detail panel, or use the **Export** page to generate AI-synthesized knowledge across sessions. See [Analytics & Export](/docs/guides/analytics-export) for details.
